title-group.article-title: Identifying commonalities and differences between EHR representations of PASC and ME/CFS in the RECOVER EHR cohort

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Shared symptoms and biological abnormalities between post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC) and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) could suggest common pathophysiological bases and would support coordinated treatment efforts. Empirical studies comparing these syndromes are needed to better understand their commonalities and differences.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We analyzed electronic health record data from 6.5 million adult patients from the National COVID Cohort Collaborative. PASC and ME/CFS diagnostic groups were defined based on recorded diagnoses, and other recorded conditions within the two groups were used to train separate machine learning-driven computable phenotypes (CPs). The most predictive conditions for each CP were examined and compared, and the overlap of patients labeled by each CP was examined. Condition records from the diagnostic groups were also used to statistically derive condition clusters. Rates of subphenotypes based on these clusters were compared between PASC and ME/CFS groups.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Approximately half of patients labeled by one CP are also labeled by the other. Dyspnea, fatigue, and cognitive impairment are the most-predictive conditions shared by both CPs, whereas other most-predictive conditions are specific to one CP. Recorded conditions separate into cardiopulmonary, neurological, and comorbidity clusters, with the cardiopulmonary cluster showing partial specificity for the PASC groups.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Data-driven approaches indicate substantial overlap in the condition records associated with PASC and ME/CFS diagnoses. Nevertheless, cardiopulmonary conditions are somewhat more commonly associated with PASC diagnosis, whereas other conditions, such as pain and sleep disturbances, are more associated with ME/CFS diagnosis. These findings suggest that symptom management approaches to these illnesses could overlap.

abstract.title: Plain language summary
abstract.p: Post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC; also known as Long COVID) and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) appear to share much in common. Understanding their similarities and differences could help to guide treatment for these complex illnesses. We analyzed data from 6.5 million adult patients from the National COVID Cohort Collaborative to evaluate patterns in their health records. We find several conditions associated with both PASC and ME/CFS diagnoses, such as difficulty breathing, fatigue, and concentration difficulties. We also find some differences. Cardiac and respiratory conditions are more typical with PASC diagnoses. Records of pain, sleep disturbances, and neuropsychiatric conditions more commonly accompany ME/CFS diagnoses. Overall, the similarities we see could support overlapping symptom management approaches across these illnesses.

Powers et al. investigate commonalities and differences between electronic health record patterns associated with post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS). Records of dyspnea, fatigue, and cognitive impairment are common diagnoses and recommend common symptom management.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The occurrence of post-acute phenomena in the aftermath of viral infections has been recognized for centuries1,2, but the emergence of post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), commonly known as Long COVID, has cast a spotlight on these illnesses, emphasizing the critical need for research into their commonalities and distinctions, as well as their underlying mechanisms. PASC can be defined as ongoing, relapsing, or new symptoms, or other health effects occurring after the acute phase of SARS-CoV-2 infection (i.e., present four or more weeks after the acute infection). Literature has particularly identified similarities between PASC and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), a complex chronic disorder affecting multiple body systems and frequently heralded by an infection, necessitating empirical studies that compare the two3,4. Such an examination is vital to advance research into PASC and related infection-associated chronic diseases.

PASC can be characterized by multi-organ effects or autoimmune conditions with symptoms lasting months to years5. One of the biggest challenges in studying PASC is the complexity of the disease as it can variously affect the respiratory, gastrointestinal, musculoskeletal, neurological, cardiovascular, and endocrine systems, as well as mental health6.

Some research indicates that approximately half of patients with PASC eventually meet criteria for ME/CFS7,8. This connection is underscored by the similarities in symptoms and proposed pathobiological mechanisms, including redox imbalance, systemic inflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction9. Thus, some patients with PASC might have ME/CFS triggered by the SARS-CoV-2 virus.

While the substantial symptom overlap and biological abnormalities between PASC and other infection-associated chronic illnesses highlight potential common pathophysiological bases10, it is important to recognize that PASC signifies any long-term symptoms after SARS-CoV-2 infection. Some patients’ pathologic features are centered around damage to specific organs or organ systems, as in cardiomyopathy or lung fibrosis11. Patients may also develop syndromic infection-associated chronic illnesses, including ME/CFS, mast cell activation syndrome, postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS), other dysautonomias, or pediatric acute-onset neuropsychiatric syndrome12,13.

Given the multifaceted nature and complexity of PASC, it is imperative to delineate and identify its various subphenotypes. Although the symptoms and diagnoses recorded in electronic health records (EHRs) involve subjectivity, systematic differences, and potential clinician biases in what information gets recorded14, they have been widely used for this purpose. A study by Reese et al. delineated six distinct categories of PASC patients, each characterized by specific abnormalities such as pulmonary, neuropsychiatric, and cardiovascular issues15. In addition, Pfaff et al. analyzed patients diagnosed with PASC and identified four major clusters of conditions that frequently accompany the PASC diagnosis code: cardiopulmonary, neurological, gastrointestinal, and comorbid conditions16. These subphenotyping methods hold promise for aiding future research in developing detailed patient profiles for PASC, thereby enhancing understanding and management of this condition.

In light of these findings, our research aims to delineate the relationship between PASC and ME/CFS in EHRs by leveraging the National COVID Cohort Collaborative (N3C), a collection of EHR data from 83 health partners representing 21.7 million patients (https://covid.cd2h.org/). Specifically, we seek to identify and compare data-driven PASC and ME/CFS phenotypes and subphenotypes in adults to contribute to a deeper understanding of these complex conditions, more precise diagnostic criteria, and better-targeted future therapeutic approaches. We develop machine learning-driven computable phenotypes (CPs) for PASC and ME/CFS. This study is part of the NIH Researching COVID to Enhance Recovery (RECOVER) Initiative, which seeks to understand, treat, and prevent PASC (https://recovercovid.org/).

In this study, approximately half of patients labeled by one CP are also labeled by the other. Both CPs identify dyspnea, fatigue, and cognitive impairment as strongly predictive conditions, while other condition records are strongly predictive of only one CP. Co-occurring condition records in patients with PASC or ME/CFS diagnoses separate into three clusters. A cardiopulmonary cluster of conditions shows partial specificity for the PASC groups. These findings indicate substantial overlap in the condition records associated with PASC and ME/CFS diagnoses, along with specific differences.

sec.title: Data
sec.p: We used EHR data integrated and harmonized in the N3C Data Enclave17. Many partnering sites contribute data to the N3C Data Enclave, which can be heterogeneous for a variety of reasons. Thus, data were excluded from sites with data quality concerns based on the following criteria: sites cannot have shifted dates by more than 30 days (i.e., sites may shift all dates in a patient’s record by a constant value of up to ±30 days to further protect patient privacy), sites must have serum creatinine and lymphocyte count results for at least 25% of hospitalizations, sites cannot have more than 10% of COVID-related hospitalizations wherein the patient’s earliest positive COVID test or diagnosis is more than 200 days after the hospitalization start date, sites must report date of birth for more than 90% of patients, and sites must have contributed new data to the Data Enclave within the past 12 months. In addition, we only included data from sites with any recorded use of ICD-10-CM diagnostic codes for both PASC (U09.9) and ME/CFS (G93.32). These criteria resulted in data from 39 sites being included in this study out of 83 sites in N3C at the time.

Data transfers to the National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences for N3C, which consist of Limited Data Sets, are performed under Johns Hopkins University Reliance Protocol #IRB00249128 or individual site agreements with the National Institutes of Health, which include waivers of informed consent. The N3C Data Enclave is managed under the authority of the National Institutes of Health; more information can be found at https://ncats.nih.gov/n3c/resources. All data preprocessing and analyses for this study were performed using SQL, Python, and R code in the Palantir Foundry platform of the N3C Data Enclave.

sec.title: Terminology
sec.p: In the N3C Data Enclave, source data from contributing sites are harmonized into the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership (OMOP) common data model. In OMOP, conditions encompass diagnoses, signs, and symptoms18. As these are the input data for our modeling and analyses, we likewise refer to these data as conditions. We use the term diagnosis to refer to a recorded diagnosis of PASC or ME/CFS. Feature is the standard term for a predictor variable in machine learning.

sec.title: Diagnostic Groups
sec.p: We defined initial patient groups based on diagnostic codes for PASC or ME/CFS. We used combinations of harmonized diagnostic codes and original source diagnostic codes, which are retained in OMOP, unless the site uses OMOP natively.

Inclusion in the PASC diagnostic group required at least two occurrences of the PASC diagnostic code on different days in the patient’s record, with the PASC diagnostic code defined as a harmonized code of OMOP5160861 (post-acute COVID-19 in the OMOP Extension vocabulary) associated with a source code of U09.9 (post-acute COVID-19 in the ICD-10-CM vocabulary), occurring after January 31, 2020. The U09.9 code was not introduced until late 2021, but providers sometimes intentionally backdate codes for greater accuracy. Backdated codes can also result from errors. Coding practices differ across contributing sites and their providers, so we cannot distinguish the reasons for backdated codes in these data. Regardless, as we report under Results below, the relative number of these backdated codes is very small. Note, multiple occurrences of a diagnostic code in a patient’s record do not imply diagnostic evaluation by more than one provider or even multiple diagnostic evaluations; nevertheless, requiring multiple occurrences of a code is a common technique for improving accuracy in identifying cases of chronic conditions in health data19,20. Index dates for patients in this group (n = 28,318) were defined as the date of the earliest PASC diagnostic code.

Inclusion in the ME/CFS diagnostic group required at least two occurrences of an ME/CFS diagnostic code on different days, with ME/CFS diagnostic codes defined as a harmonized code of 52702003 (chronic fatigue syndrome in the Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine-Clinical Terminology [SNOMED CT] vocabulary) associated with a source code of G93.32 (myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome in the ICD-10-CM vocabulary) or R53.82 (chronic fatigue, unspecified in the ICD-10-CM vocabulary). No date restriction was applied. Additionally, inclusion in the ME/CFS group required at least one of the ME/CFS diagnostic codes to be associated with the more specific G93.32 source code. Index dates for patients in this group (n = 5781) were defined as the date of the earliest G93.32 code. Inclusion in the ME/CFS and PASC groups was determined independently, allowing for the possibility of patients being included in both groups.

Although we required a G93.32 code occurrence for inclusion in the ME/CFS diagnostic group and determination of index date, the number of occurrences of G93.32 in our dataset was small compared to R53.82, a code that does not specifically indicate ME/CFS but has frequently been used in association with ME/CFS, especially prior to the introduction of G93.32 in late 202221. Therefore, we defined another group of patients who did not meet the above criteria for the ME/CFS or PASC groups, but had at least one occurrence of a harmonized code of 52702003 associated with a source code of R53.82, which we refer to as the R53.82 group. Index dates for patients in this group (n = 146,424) were defined as the date of the earliest R53.82 code. No date restriction was applied.

Finally, a control group was defined from the remaining patients not included in any of the above groups. Inclusion in the control group required no occurrences of the above diagnostic codes for PASC or ME/CFS. Index dates for patients in this group (n = 6,396,760) were defined as the date of a random condition occurrence from their record. No date restriction was applied.

In addition to the above criteria, inclusion in any of these groups required a non-null birth date, recorded sex of female or male, and an age of at least 18 years at index date. The Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI) was computed for patients relative to index date. CCI was computed using the ICD-10 codes from Quan et al., 200522 and updated condition category weights from Quan et al., 201123. Only patients for whom CCI could be computed were included, which also required that the patient’s data come from a site where greater than 80% of condition records were recorded using ICD-10 codes.

sec.title: Harmonized Computable Phenotypes
sec.p: We and other colleagues previously developed a machine learning-driven CP of PASC using EHR data in the N3C Data Enclave24, which has been recently updated25,26. Using this CP as a foundation, we developed a pair of harmonized CP models, one for PASC and one for ME/CFS. We describe our modeling steps below, but additional details regarding these steps can be found in these previous publications.

Training datasets were defined for each model by first randomly sampling the diagnostic group of interest and then combining those patients with a subset of patients of equal size from the control group exactly matched on decade-binned age, sex, race, ethnicity, and CCI score. All condition records were selected for these patients within a 100-day time window from 70 days prior to the patient’s index date to 30 days after the index date, except for condition records from one week before to four weeks after onset of acute COVID-19 infections. This exclusion helps prevent the model from mistaking conditions associated with acute COVID-19 for conditions associated with PASC or ME/CFS diagnoses. OMOP primarily uses the SNOMED CT for conditions rather than ICD-10. Uncommon conditions were rolled up to more common parent conditions based on the SNOMED CT hierarchy, following the method of the previous PASC CP model25, with the addition of combining conditions related to pregnancy and delivery. Conditions specifically related to diagnoses of PASC, ME/CFS, or COVID-19 were excluded.

Binary variables were created indicating the presence or absence of each condition for each patient, and a covariance value was calculated for each condition with group label (PASC or ME/CFS vs. control). We explored the possibility of using ternary variables instead, capturing absence versus one occurrence versus multiple occurrences of each condition, but model performances were not improved relative to models using the simpler binary variables. The 200 conditions with the largest magnitude of covariance (positive or negative) with each group label were then combined into a master list of 297 conditions of interest. This master list of conditions was combined with decade-binned age and sex for a final list of 299 features. The inclusion of conditions with negative covariance represents a deviation from the previous PASC CP model. Including these conditions gives the models more opportunity to learn when conditions that can be related to PASC or ME/CFS may be better explained by other unrelated conditions.

Both training datasets were reduced to the shared final list of features and then used to train separate gradient-boosted decision tree ensemble models for PASC and ME/CFS using the XGBoost package (https://xgboost.readthedocs.io/en/release_1.4.0/) in Python. Each model outputs a probability score for the label of interest (PASC or ME/CFS) for each patient. To convert these model scores into the presence or absence of a CP requires selection of a threshold, with the typical default being 0.5. One common approach to selecting a threshold is maximizing Youden’s J, the difference between true positive rate and false positive rate27. This metric has the desirable property of being independent of the true base rate of the condition, which is not known precisely for ME/CFS or PASC. For the PASC CP model, Youden’s J achieved a maximum value of 0.534 at a threshold of 0.495, while for the ME/CFS CP model, Youden’s J achieved a maximum value of 0.544 at a threshold of 0.414. A threshold of 0.5 yielded very similar Youden’s J values of 0.533 and 0.528, respectively (with potential bias toward greater specificity). Thus, 0.5 was selected as the standard threshold for both models.

Regarding final model performance, area under the receiver operating characteristic curve was 0.841 and 0.842 for the PASC and ME/CFS CP models, respectively. Thus, both models demonstrated similar predictive performance for a range of thresholds. We computed the sensitivities of these CP models for various diagnostic groups, which are reported under Results.

To examine which features had the largest impact on the CP model predictions, i.e., which features the models learned were most useful for classifying PASC or ME/CFS versus control, we identified a set of most-predictive features for each CP. These most-predictive features only included conditions; therefore, for clarity, we hereafter refer to these as most-predictive conditions. We used the SHAP package (https://shap.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) in Python to compute Shapley values for each patient and feature in the training datasets for each model. For binary features, the sign of the Shapley value was reversed when the feature was absent, and then the mean was computed, yielding a feature importance score. By including the sign-reversal step, the sign of the final importance score indicates whether the presence of the feature had an overall negative or positive impact on model prediction scores. Most-predictive conditions were defined as conditions with positive importance scores of at least 0.05, indicating the presence of the condition had an overall impact of at least 0.05 on the CP label probability scores.

sec.title: Identifying Subphenotypes
sec.p: Subphenotyping began with a data-driven, statistical method for establishing clusters of relevant conditions, which we and other colleagues have recently published16. This method identified condition records that frequently co-occurred with PASC or ME/CFS diagnoses in the EHR data and then clustered these conditions based on how they co-occurred with each other.

We first defined a combined group of patients balanced for PASC and ME/CFS diagnoses using the diagnostic groups, which we refer to as the cluster definition group. The PASC diagnostic group was larger than the ME/CFS diagnostic group and therefore had to be reduced to achieve a balanced, combined group. Thus, we combined all patients who were in both diagnostic groups (n = 1634), all patients who were in only the ME/CFS group (n = 3897), and a random sample of equal size of patients who were in only the PASC group. We began with condition data as defined in Harmonized Computable Phenotypes above: condition records from 70 days before to 30 days after index date, with uncommon conditions rolled up to more common conditions. We identified conditions that occurred in the records of at least five percent of the patients in the group, yielding 46 conditions for clustering. Similar to the p-value convention of 0.05 in statistics, this threshold was selected to capture conditions that were typical in the data.

An adjacency matrix was constructed from these condition records, with values indicating the frequency of co-occurrence (within patient) of records for each pair of conditions. This matrix was used to construct a weighted network with individual conditions as nodes, co-occurrence as edges, and edge weights corresponding to the frequency of co-occurrence. We used the Louvain algorithm28 to separate these conditions into clusters based on their patterns of co-occurrence using the igraph library in R.

Using these clusters of conditions, we characterized the diagnostic groups and the groups defined by CP labels, which we refer to as the CP groups. We divided the diagnostic groups into patients in both the PASC and ME/CFS groups, patients only in the PASC group, and patients only in the ME/CFS group (total N = 29,613). We performed a similar division of patients for the CP groups (total N = 1,825,750). For both sets of patients, we generated heatmaps indicating the percentage of patients with condition data consistent with each cluster, which was determined using cluster scores. To generate cluster scores for each patient, each condition within a cluster was assigned a weight based on the proportion of the patient count in the cluster definition group with data for that condition versus the sum of these patient counts for all conditions in the cluster. Thus, a weight corresponds to how representative a condition is of a cluster based on the frequency of that condition in the cluster definition group, and the weights of all conditions within each cluster sum to one. Cluster scores were then computed for each patient and cluster by summing the weights of all the conditions for which the patient had data. Patients were considered to meet criteria for any clusters for which they had a cluster score of at least 0.2. This threshold was selected to balance the rate of patients not meeting the criteria for any cluster with the notion that meeting criteria for a many-condition cluster should generally require having data for multiple conditions in that cluster. Only one condition (dyspnea) had a weight greater than 0.2; thus, meeting this threshold generally required data for multiple conditions. We also generated heatmaps indicating the percentage of patients meeting criteria for subphenotypes based on all possible combinations of the condition clusters.

sec.title: Reporting summary
sec.p: Further information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Regarding the demographics and social determinants of the patient groups (see Supplementary Data 1), one of the most prominent results is the higher proportion of patients who identify as female in all other diagnostic groups relative to the control group, especially the ME/CFS group. The proportion of patients who identify as Black or African American is lower in the PASC and ME/CFS diagnostic groups compared to the control group, and the proportion of patients who identify as White is higher; these differences are larger for the ME/CFS group. In addition, the proportion of patients in the ME/CFS diagnostic group who identify as not Hispanic or Latino is higher in comparison to the PASC and control groups, and the category of not Hispanic or Latino has a higher positivity rate for the ME/CFS CP relative to the other ethnicity categories.

In Fig. 1, we present longitudinal trends in the index dates of the diagnostic groups. Most diagnostic cases come from between late 2021 and early 2023, as expected given that the ICD-10-CM codes U09.9 and G93.32 were officially introduced in late 2021 and late 2022, respectively.Fig. 1Longitudinal trends in index dates of diagnostic groups.
a Counts of index dates for diagnostic groups over time, binned by quarter. In this panel, “PASC_ME/CFS” (red dashed and dotted line) indicates patients who met criteria for the PASC and ME/CFS diagnostic groups, while “PASC” (blue solid line) and “ME/CFS” (green dashed line) indicate patients who met criteria only for that given group. b Cumulative counts of index dates for diagnostic groups over time, binned by quarter. In this panel, lines indicate all patients who met criteria for that diagnostic group, i.e., patients in both groups are included in both lines.

For the ME/CFS CP, patients in the ME/CFS diagnostic group are labeled at a rate of 71.6% versus 52.5% for patients in the PASC diagnostic group (see Supplementary Table 1). Notably, patients in the R53.82 group are labeled at a rate of 56.7%, between the other groups but closer to the PASC group’s rate. All these rates far exceed the rate for patients designated as controls based on diagnostic codes, which is 16.9%. Thus, the ME/CFS CP clearly differentiates between diagnostic ME/CFS cases versus controls and demonstrates some specificity relative to the other diagnostic groups. While comparing these rates, it is important to keep in mind that the PASC and ME/CFS diagnostic groups are not mutually exclusive and the rates of undiagnosed PASC or ME/CFS in this dataset are not known. These limitations preclude the use of metrics relying on concepts of true and false positives and negatives. For the PASC CP, patients in the PASC diagnostic group are labeled at a rate of 68.0% versus 53.0% for patients in the ME/CFS group. Patients in the R53.82 group are labeled at a rate of 38.2%, much lower than the other groups, but not as low as the control group rate of 13.0%. Thus, the PASC CP also differentiates between diagnostic groups as expected.

In the diagnostic groups, only considering index dates from October 1, 2022, onward (when all relevant diagnostic codes were available), 6.9% of patients in the PASC group are also in the ME/CFS group, and 33.0% of patients in the ME/CFS group are also in the PASC group (Fig. 2a and Supplementary Table 2). However, patients did not have equal opportunities to be included in each of these groups due to various biases that cannot be quantified here (see Discussion). The CPs consider all records equally and make for a better comparison; the diagnostic group results are only included for the sake of comparison with the CP group results. In the CP groups, only considering index dates from July 1, 2020, onward (a time period in which both illnesses can be expected to exist in these data), 60.1% of patients in the PASC group are also in the ME/CFS group, and 47.1% of patients in the ME/CFS group are also in the PASC group (Fig. 2b and Supplementary Table 3). Thus, the overlap in patients labeled by the PASC and ME/CFS CPs is substantial but not complete.Fig. 2Overlap of patient groups.
a Patient overlap in diagnostic groups, with index dates restricted to October 1, 2022 onward. b Patient overlap in CP groups, with index dates restricted to July 1, 2020 onward. Blue shading = PASC only; red shading = overlap / in both groups; green shading = ME/CFS only.

The partial overlap in CP labels corresponds with examination of the most predictive conditions learned by the PASC and ME/CFS CP models (see Fig. 3 and Supplementary Table 4). Three most-predictive conditions emerge for both CPs (dyspnea, fatigue, and finding related to attentiveness), indicating records of these conditions are important for determining a CP label for both PASC and ME/CFS. Notably, these are the three most predictive conditions for the PASC CP, with dyspnea being the single most predictive by far, and they are three of the six most predictive conditions for the ME/CFS CP, with fatigue being the single most important condition for this CP. On the other hand, three additional most-predictive conditions are only identified for the PASC CP, and 12 for the ME/CFS CP, indicating some generally less predictive conditions are more specific to one of the CP labels.Fig. 3Comparison of most-predictive conditions for the PASC and ME/CFS CPs.
An importance score threshold of 0.05 was used to define most-predictive conditions for each CP. Red bubbles indicate conditions that meet this criterion for both CPs, blue bubbles for PASC only, and green bubbles for ME/CFS only. Note different ranges on axes. Bubble sizes are proportional to the percentage of patients in the cluster definition group with a record of the given condition. Condition names are abbreviated. Abbreviations follow, but full names can also be found in Supplementary Table 4. Attention = Finding related to attentiveness; C. = chronic; Depressive = Depressive disorder; GAD = Generalized anxiety disorder; Hypothyr. = Hypothyroidism; Immune = Disorder of immune function; OSAS = Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome; Smell/taste = Sensory disorder of smell and/or taste; syn. = syndrome; Vit B. = Vitamin B deficiency; Vit. D = Vitamin D deficiency.

As a test of the robustness of these similarity results, a supporting model was trained to directly distinguish between the EHRs of patients with PASC and ME/CFS diagnoses in the same manner as the separate PASC and ME/CFS CP models. This model performs poorly at distinguishing between patients diagnosed with only PASC and patients diagnosed with only ME/CFS (area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.63). This result suggests that the records of patients with only a PASC or ME/CFS diagnosis are only moderately separable using EHR condition data, supporting the overlap observed between the independent CPs. Analysis of the model highlights no additional conditions for potentially distinguishing PASC and ME/CFS.

In terms of characterizing the condition records for these patients, which can include both symptoms and comorbidities, clustering analysis yields 46 common conditions that separate into three clusters (see Fig. 4 and Supplementary Data 2). Cluster labels are adopted from the similar clusters found in a related previous study; these labels were developed by a clinician review team by examining the contents of the clusters16. These labels are post-hoc descriptions of convenience, and they are not meant to be interpreted as definitive categorizations of all the contents in each cluster or to specifically address their underlying pathophysiologic basis. The cardiopulmonary and neurological clusters mostly contain conditions that could be considered symptoms of PASC or ME/CFS, while the comorbidity cluster contains many comorbidities common to adult EHR. A heatmap of these condition clusters in the diagnostic groups (Fig. 5a) reveals the largest discrepancy between PASC and ME/CFS in the cardiopulmonary cluster, with this cluster more common in the PASC diagnostic group. The rates of the other clusters are much closer between these diagnostic groups. Similarly, a heatmap of subphenotypes based on exclusive cluster combinations (Fig. 5b) shows the largest discrepancy between the PASC and ME/CFS diagnostic groups in patients who only meet criteria for the cardiopulmonary cluster, with this subphenotype being more specific to the PASC group. These effects are echoed and even more pronounced in the heatmaps for the CP groups (Fig. 5c, d). To a lesser degree, these CP group heatmaps also indicate some specificity of the neurological and comorbidity clusters for patients labeled with the ME/CFS CP.Fig. 4Condition clusters.
Clusters indicate patterns of condition record co-occurrence in a combined PASC and ME/CFS group. Clusters are denoted by box color with blue boxes corresponding to the neurological cluster, yellow boxes to the cardiopulmonary cluster, and brown boxes to the comorbidity cluster. Box sizes correspond to the weight of each condition within its cluster. Condition names are abbreviated. Abbreviations follow, but full names can also be found in Supplementary Data 2. Ab. ECG = Electrocardiogram abnormal; Ab. lung imaging = Abnormal findings on diagnostic imaging of lung; Anxiety = Anxiety disorder; Asthma = Uncomplicated asthma; Atherosclerosis = Atherosclerosis of coronary artery without angina pectoris; Attention = Finding related to attentiveness; Blood chem. = Blood chemistry outside reference range; C. = chronic; COPD = Chronic obstructive lung disease; Depression episode = Major depression, single episode; Depressive = Depressive disorder; Dizziness = Dizziness and giddiness; GAD = Generalized anxiety disorder; GERD w/o esophagitis = Gastroesophageal reflux disease without esophagitis; Hyperlipidemia = Mixed hyperlipidemia; Hypertension = Essential hypertension; Hypothyr. = Hypothyroidism; OSAS = Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome; Postop. state = Postoperative state; Type 2 DM = Type 2 diabetes mellitus; Type 2 DM w/o complication = Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complication; Vit. D = Vitamin D deficiency.Fig. 5Condition cluster and subphenotype rates across patient groups.
a Proportion of patients in each diagnostic group meeting criteria for each condition cluster. b Proportion of patients in each diagnostic group meeting criteria for each subphenotype. c Proportion of patients in each CP group meeting criteria for each condition cluster. d Proportion of patients in each CP group meeting criteria for each subphenotype. “PASC_ME/CFS” indicates patients in the PASC and ME/CFS groups, while “PASC” and “ME/CFS” indicate patients only in that group. Subphenotype categories are applied exclusively, e.g., a subphenotype of “1” indicates patients who meet criteria for cluster 1 only. In contrast, the cluster heatmaps indicate all patients who meet criteria for each cluster, e.g., cluster 1 includes patients from the “1,” “1 & 2,” “1 & 3,” and “All” subphenotypes. Darker shading corresponds to higher proportions. Cluster 1 = neurological cluster; Cluster 2 = comorbidity cluster; Cluster 3 = cardiopulmonary cluster.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Complementing previous literature reviews comparing symptom patterns of PASC and ME/CFS2–4, we leverage machine learning models and other data-driven approaches using a large set of real-world adult patient data to compare EHR representations of these two disorders. Overall, our findings indicate considerable overlap in EHR profiles of PASC and ME/CFS, as well as some distinctive features. The main findings of this study stem from the development and analysis of machine learning-based computable phenotypes (CPs) for PASC and ME/CFS and condition-based subphenotypes.

Examining the features learned by the CP models reveals the specific conditions in the EHR that best distinguish patients with diagnoses of these syndromes versus patients without these diagnoses. While dyspnea is the single strongest predictor in the PASC CP and fatigue in the ME/CFS CP, both are most-predictive conditions in both CPs. The other most-predictive condition shared by both CPs is “finding related to attentiveness,” which could more generally be referred to as a cognitive impairment. These shared most-predictive conditions are also the three most predictive conditions for the PASC CP and perfectly match the three most debilitating symptoms reported by patients with PASC in a large survey29. For ME/CFS, fatigue and cognitive impairment are part of the 2015 Institute of Medicine (now U.S. National Academy of Medicine) diagnostic criteria30. Although not considered a central feature of ME/CFS, dyspnea is included among the optional criteria of the earlier International Consensus Criteria31 and Canadian Consensus Criteria32 (see Supplementary Table 5 for further consideration of dyspnea and ME/CFS in this dataset). This alignment of most-predictive CP conditions with self-reported symptoms and diagnostic criteria provides strong support for the validity of these models, which identified these conditions through fully data-driven methods as opposed to surveys manually curated to assess known symptoms.

Several more conditions are only most-predictive conditions for one CP. For the PASC CP, these include another respiratory condition as well as cardiac and sensory (specifically smell and taste) conditions, all of which have been reported as symptoms of PASC4, and disorders of smell or taste have been previously recognized as specific to PASC3. The list of most-predictive conditions specific to the ME/CFS CP is longer and more expansive; it includes vitamin deficiencies, immune dysfunction, neuropsychiatric conditions, pain conditions, hypothyroidism, and sleep conditions (overlapping with previous machine-learning modeling results33). These conditions have been recognized as symptoms and clinical findings in ME/CFS34, although in some cases, neuropsychiatric conditions may be misdiagnosed in patients with ME/CFS35. Similarly, diagnosis of hypothyroidism could be the result of confusion of symptoms in the process of diagnosing ME/CFS36–38, but it has also been reported as a comorbidity39. While less commonly recognized, vitamin deficiencies have been observed in patients with ME/CFS40–43, particularly B vitamins, vitamin D, and iron, which may be due to immune response to initial infection or ongoing immune activation that has been characterized as anemia of inflammation or chronic disease44. Being most-predictive conditions in these CP models does not imply a causal relationship between these conditions and PASC or ME/CFS but indicates that their presence is observed more often in the records of patients with diagnoses of PASC or ME/CFS versus control patients matched on several demographic characteristics and overall comorbidity burden.

Clustering the most common conditions present in the records of patients with PASC or ME/CFS and subphenotyping based on these clusters serves a somewhat different purpose than examining the most predictive CP conditions. While the CP condition examination could be framed as identifying which EHR condition data are most predictive of PASC or ME/CFS diagnosis, the subphenotyping analyses characterize common EHR condition profiles in these patients. The most pronounced difference between PASC and ME/CFS from these analyses is that the cardiopulmonary cluster presents more commonly in the records of patients with a PASC diagnosis or the PASC CP label. This could be expected given that this cluster contains three of the five most predictive conditions for the PASC CP: palpitations, chronic cough, and dyspnea, which is also the most representative condition of this cluster. Although not as strong, there is also some evidence of the other clusters presenting more commonly in the records of patients with an ME/CFS diagnosis or the ME/CFS CP label. This is more notable for the subphenotype of the comorbidity cluster presenting alone, which resembles how the most-predictive conditions specific to the ME/CFS CP include a broad spectrum of conditions. The neurological cluster contains fatigue, which is the most predictive condition of the ME/CFS CP and the most representative condition of this cluster, but it is also the second most important condition of the PASC CP, making it less specific.

From the diagnostic and treatment perspectives, there may be meaningful and more efficient ways to organize some of the conditions included in these models and analyses, including ways that better align with current understandings of these illnesses. For example, concepts like dysautonomia, orthostatic intolerance, and POTS could be used to group numerous symptoms, and there may be value in considering symptoms collectively under diagnoses like these in the context of these illnesses. Such directions would be valuable to future research; however, the present work fundamentally employs data-driven methods to discover patterns inherent to the data as they exist, without differential treatments of the data such as manual categorizations.

Our ability to estimate the rates of overlap between patients with PASC and patients with ME/CFS is limited in this study. Accurate use of EHR-documented diagnoses for these estimates would require equating the PASC and ME/CFS groups for factors such as changes in clinician recognition of these illnesses over time and changes in the adoption and use of diagnostic codes over time, which are not known for these data. Without accounting for these factors, we find 6.9% of patients in the PASC diagnostic group are also in the ME/CFS diagnostic group. In contrast, we find a much higher rate using the CP groups (60.1%), which highlights the similarity of the EHR condition profiles learned by the CPs.

Using structured EHR data allows us to leverage the large patient dataset of N3C and analytic methods developed specifically for these data through the NIH RECOVER Initiative, but it also entails limitations. Many patients meeting the criteria for PASC or ME/CFS may not have these diagnoses in their EHRs14,16,41. Thus, we developed CPs to identify patients similar to those with diagnoses, based on recorded conditions. Also, index dates were defined to approximate when patients first met criteria for PASC or ME/CFS, and analyses were restricted to a fixed time interval around those dates. These steps are important for identifying condition records with the strongest temporal associations with PASC or ME/CFS diagnoses in a standardized way across all patients. However, given that EHR data in N3C are not comprehensive and the specific ICD-10-CM code for ME/CFS (G93.32) was only recently introduced, these index dates likely do not always reflect true syndrome onset. The frequency with which the analytic window actually captures a later stage of illness, particularly for ME/CFS, is unknown. Thus, differences observed between PASC and ME/CFS could be influenced by differences in pre-index illness duration. Relatedly, if a patient arrives at a clinical encounter with a previous record of one of these diagnoses, the provider could be biased to attend to and record its known associated symptoms. Such confirmation biases could also vary by the type and expertise of the provider, which should be explored by future work. Disparities in access to healthcare and biases in healthcare interactions can also influence the condition records in patients’ EHRs14,16,45, leading to the potential for collider bias46. To help attenuate this concern, we employed strict case-control matching in CP development. Nevertheless, it is important to note that previous research using N3C data has found that the population of patients with a PASC diagnosis is demographically skewed toward female, White, non-Hispanic individuals living in communities with low poverty and low unemployment43,44 (see also for ME/CFS47,48). EHR data involving PASC or ME/CFS and any CPs developed from those data can incorporate clinician biases and are unlikely to be truly representative of all individuals with PASC or ME/CFS. We emphasize that lower diagnostic rates in Black, Indigenous, and other People of Color in this dataset do not necessarily indicate lower rates of illness49,50.

Other limitations include that there is no ICD-10-CM code for post-exertional malaise, so it was not directly accounted for in analyses. Given that post-exertional malaise is often considered the hallmark symptom of ME/CFS51 and it could have implications for differential treatment approaches to PASC, ME/CFS, or particular subphenotypes, future research on these topics would benefit greatly from the creation and adoption of a dedicated code. Similarly, orthostatic intolerance is an important symptom pattern in these illnesses52–54, and while its manifestation as POTS now has an ICD-10-CM code, its introduction was too recent to be appropriately incorporated into our modeling. Nevertheless, tachycardia and palpitations more generally did emerge in our findings. More broadly, condition records are just one potential data source for phenotyping. Additional data such as clinical notes and laboratory results could enrich phenotypes in future work.

Using multiple approaches to define and characterize EHR representations of PASC and ME/CFS in adults, we find substantial overlap in the condition records associated with these diagnoses along with some distinct patterns. Key overlapping conditions include dyspnea, fatigue, and cognitive impairment. Other conditions and subphenotypes were more frequently associated with either PASC or ME/CFS diagnosis. Limitations in EHR data warrant care in interpreting these findings, but overall they suggest substantial overlap in these illnesses that could support exploring shared treatment approaches.

sec.title: Peer review information
sec.p: Communications Medicine thanks Carl Wahlgren, Vikki McKeever and the other, anonymous, reviewer(s) for their contribution to the peer review of this work.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: All datasets used in this study, including source data for figures, are stored in the N3C Data Enclave. The N3C Data Enclave is managed under the authority of the National Institutes of Health. Data in the Enclave are protected and can be accessed for research related to COVID 19 with an approved institutional review board protocol, data use agreement, and data use request. More information can be found at https://ncats.nih.gov/n3c/resources.

notes.title: Code availability
notes.p: All code used to produce the analyses in this manuscript is available within the N3C Data Enclave to users with valid login credentials to support reproducibility. The data use request ID for this study within the Enclave is RP-5677B5. The code is also available at 10.17605/OSF.IO/G7RJW55 Software versions: igraph 1.4.2, MatchIt 4.1.0, PySpark 3.2.1.60, Python 3.6.10, R 3.5.3 and 4.1.3, SHAP 0.39.0, XGBoost 1.4.2
